one_line_takeaway: PRISM reframes backdoor defense as an external, VLM-based semantic audit rather than internal model diagnosis, and across 17 datasets/11 attacks it is the only tested data-free defense that suppresses CIFAR-10 average ASR to under 1% while improving clean accuracy, though full auditor-victim collusion (identical trigger and target embedded in both models) still fully defeats it.
key_finding: On the CIFAR-10 main comparison (Table 1, averaged across all 11 attacks), No Defense gives CA 92.0%/ASR 96.4%; the strongest prior defenses only partially suppress ASR (e.g., CLP: CA 90.1%/ASR 34.6%; LPP: CA 89.3%/ASR 28.8%; Refine, the strongest baseline: CA 86.9%/ASR 14.3%; naive Zero-shot VLM: CA 86.7%/ASR 0.4% but with catastrophic CA drop, e.g. >=70% drop on GTSRB elsewhere in the paper). PRISM achieves CA 93.2%/ASR 0.8% on average, and is stated as the only method suppressing ASR below 15% on every one of the 11 attacks while simultaneously improving CA (CA rises +1.2 vs. No Defense) -- notably it is the only defense that handles the Clean-Image attacks FLIP (ASR 1.1% vs. baselines mostly 28-100%) and GCB (ASR 4.4% vs. most baselines at or near 100%) where every other tested defense (including Refine) fails. Across 17 datasets (Table 2) PRISM defends Blend/BPP/CTRL to near-0% ASR even under extreme class imbalance (Caltech101, IR=86.5) and very low VLM zero-shot accuracy (13.4% on SVHN), while sometimes improving CA over the raw victim (e.g., +33.4pp CA on STL10-Blend). Ablation (Table 3) shows removing Online Update spikes GTSRB Classic ASR from 1.3% to 32.9%; removing Prototype Refinement drops GTSRB Classic CA by up to ~20pp (from 94.4% down to ~86.1% across categories) though ASR stays low; the hyperparameter zeta=-2 is shown empirically optimal across all datasets/backbones only when Skewness Correction (Cornish-Fisher) is applied -- without it the optimal threshold varies dataset-to-dataset (e.g., -2 for CIFAR vs -3 for GTSRB). Under adaptive online attacks (Flooding/Periodic/Mixed) ASR stays below 20% with CA drop under 5%. Under the auditor-aware typographic attack, PRISM's ASR rises from 1.3% to 15.6% (vs. Zero-shot VLM alone rising to 60.1% ASR), showing the defense degrades but does not fully break. VLM-collusion tests (Table 5) show only full trigger+target collusion (Sc3) breaks the defense (ASR 100%); partial collusion scenarios Sc1/Sc2 cause negligible degradation (ASR <=0.8%). Latency: PRISM with CLIP backbone runs at 12.5ms/image on an A100, ranking 2nd-fastest among 9 tested defenses.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is PRISM (Prototype Refinement & Inspection via Statistical Monitoring), a data-free, online, test-time backdoor defense that wraps a potentially compromised 'victim' classifier with an independent, frozen Vision-Language Model (VLM) acting as an external semantic auditor, rather than diagnosing the victim model's internal weights/activations. PRISM has three pieces, all attributed to this paper: (1) a Dual Stream Inference framework running the victim model and a frozen VLM teacher in parallel on each test sample; (2) a Hybrid VLM Teacher that fuses static text-anchor similarity (Stext) with online-refined visual class-prototype similarity (Sproto) via a weighted sum SVLM = lambda*Sproto + (1-lambda)*Stext, where prototypes are class centroids initialized empty and updated only from samples the router certifies clean; and (3) an Adaptive Router that computes a logit-margin discrepancy Delta = exp(S_yhatS_VLM - max_{i!=yhatS} S_i_VLM) between the victim's predicted class and the VLM's next-best class, and accepts/rejects the victim prediction against a per-class adaptive threshold tau_k = mu_k + zeta_tilde_k * sigma_k, where the skewness-adjusted coefficient zeta_tilde_k = zeta + (gamma_k/6)(zeta^2 - 1) is derived from a Cornish-Fisher expansion over online-estimated mean/variance/skewness of Delta, updated via Cumulative Moving Average (CMA) rather than EMA, and warmed up with a Gaussian prior (gamma=0) linearly blended toward the skewness-aware form as N approaches ~100 samples. The paper's stated novelty claim, contrasted explicitly against its own Related Work section, is being 'the first use of VLM for data-free backdoor defense without requiring training interventions or access to model weights' and the first to combine universal VLM semantics with online statistical monitoring for test-time defense refinement -- this is distinguished in-text from prior VLM-for-security work (VLM as attack target, VLM for training-time defense, VLM for federated-learning backdoor mitigation) which the paper places in Related Work, not in its own contribution.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Sec 3.2 ('Threat Model'): a stringent data-free, test-time setting where the defender deploys a potentially compromised 'suspicious model' fS with NO access to any labeled reference/training data. The adversary may have embedded arbitrary backdoors -- including stealthy clean-label, dynamic, or clean-image (natural-trigger) backdoors -- into fS via data poisoning or training-process manipulation. The defender's only tool is a public, pre-trained, clean, frozen VLM used as an external auditor; the VLM is assumed statistically independent of the victim's poisoned distribution and is never updated/fine-tuned, which the paper argues eliminates the attack surface for test-time weight manipulation of the auditor itself. The paper also separately stress-tests two escalations beyond this base threat model as adaptive-adversary experiments: (a) an 'auditor-aware' adversary that manipulates inputs with adversarial typography/text to fool the VLM directly (Sec 5.5.2), and (b) a 'VLM collusion' adversary that additionally backdoors the auditing VLM itself, evaluated across four coordination scenarios Sc0-Sc3 (Table 5), with the paper's own finding that only full collusion (same trigger AND same target embedded in both victim and auditor) breaks the defense.
limitations: The paper's own Discussion section (Sec 6) states two explicit weaknesses: (1) for domain-specific tasks where public VLMs have low zero-shot coverage (e.g., 3D perception, industrial state estimation), audit signal quality degrades, for which they propose a 'Trust Chain' mitigation (App. E) rather than a solved fix; (2) full VLM-collusion (Sc3, both victim and auditor poisoned with the same trigger/target) breaks the defense entirely (100% ASR), though the paper argues this requires a qualitatively stronger supply-chain-level adversary. Additionally the paper concedes: prototype refinement assumes the test stream contains enough clean samples to seed uncontaminated centroids during a 'dirty cold-start' window, for which only 'transient protection' via text anchors is offered (App. P); the auditor-aware typographic attack raises PRISM's ASR from 1.3% to 15.6% (a real, non-trivial degradation, not fully neutralized); and extending the logit-margin gating mechanism to non-classification tasks (forecasting, speech separation, code generation) is left as a 'preliminary design' in an appendix, not a validated result in the main paper.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10 average across 11 attacks: PRISM CA 93.2% / ASR 0.8% vs. No Defense CA 92.0% / ASR 96.4% (Table 1)

CIFAR-10 Clean-Image GCB attack: PRISM ASR 4.4% vs. every other tested defense at or near 100% ASR (CLP 100%, ScaleUp 100%, BDMAE 100%, ZIP 100%, TeCo 100%, C&C 100%, LPP 69.4%, Refine 100%) (Table 1)
CIFAR-10 Clean-Image FLIP attack: PRISM ASR 1.1% vs. strongest baseline Refine ASR 30.3% (Table 1)
Best prior defense baseline overall: Refine, average CA 86.9% / ASR 14.3% (Table 1)
Naive Zero-shot VLM baseline: average CA 86.7% / ASR 0.4%, but with severe CA collapse noted elsewhere (>=70% CA drop on GTSRB)
PRISM latency 12.5 ms/image on CLIP backbone, A100 GPU, batch size 1, ranked 2nd of 9 defenses (Sec 5.2.1, Fig. 2)
SVHN: VLM zero-shot accuracy only 13.4% yet PRISM defends effectively (Table 2)
Ablation: removing Online Update raises GTSRB-Classic ASR from 1.3% to 32.9% (Table 3)
Ablation: removing Prototype Refinement drops GTSRB-Classic CA from 94.4% to 86.1% (Table 3)
Adaptive online attacks (Flooding/Periodic/Mixed): ASR stays below 20%, CA drop below 5% (Sec 5.5.1, Fig. 6/7)
Auditor-aware typography attack: PRISM ASR rises from 1.3% (original) to 15.6% (adaptive), vs. bare Zero-shot VLM rising to 60.1% ASR (Table 4)
VLM collusion Sc3 (same trigger, same target in both victim and auditor): CA 93.7% / ASR 100%; Sc1/Sc2 partial collusion: ASR <=0.8% (Table 5)
Optimal base threshold zeta=-2 consistent across all datasets and 2 VLM backbones only when Cornish-Fisher skewness correction is applied (Fig. 5)
